blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VI
\(\ell\)-ADIC COHOMOLOGYby J. P. JOUANOLOU
Throughout the exposé, we fix a prime number \(\ell\).
As usual, we denote by \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) the étale site of a prescheme \(X\).
1. Constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaves
We fix once and for all in this paragraph a locally noetherian prescheme \(X\).
1.1. Definition and generalities

blocks.title: Definition 1.1.1.
blocks.html: Definition 1.1.1. — We call a constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaf on \(X\) a \((\ell\mathbb Z)\)-adic projective system of constructible abelian sheaves on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) (V 3.2.1).

For a \((\ell\mathbb Z)\)-adic projective system \(F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) of abelian sheaves on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\) to be a constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaf, it suffices that \(F_0\) be constructible. Indeed the assertion is local and follows in the noetherian case from (V 5.1.2).
Let us denote by \(\operatorname{Abc}(X)\) the category of constructible abelian sheaves on \(X\). We denote by
\[\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(X)\]
the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Abc}(X))\) generated by the constructible
\(\ell\)-adic sheaves. With the notations of Exp. V:
\[\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(X)=(\ell\mathbb Z)\text{-}\operatorname{ad}(\operatorname{Abc}(X)).\]

Denoting by \(\operatorname{Ab}(X)\) the category of abelian sheaves on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\), we know (SGA 4 IX 2.9) that when \(X\) is noetherian the constructible abelian sheaves on \(X\) are the noetherian abelian sheaves. In this case, the category \(\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(X)\) clearly identifies with the category denoted
\[(\ell\mathbb Z)\text{-}\operatorname{adn}(\operatorname{Ab}(X))\]
in Exp. V.

blocks.title: Proposition 1.1.2.
blocks.html: Proposition 1.1.2. — Let \(F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) be an object of the category \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\). For \(F\) to be constructible \(\ell\)-adic, it is necessary and sufficient that it be so locally for the étale topology of \(X\).

Proof: It is clear that the property for \(F\) of being \(\ell\)-adic can be read above a covering of the final object of the site. Moreover, it follows from (SGA 4 IX 2.8) that the \(F_n\) are constructible if they are so locally.

blocks.title: Proposition 1.1.3.
blocks.html: Proposition 1.1.3. — The category \(\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(X)\) of constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaves of \(X\) is abelian. It is noetherian when \(X\) is noetherian.
Proof: When \(X\) is noetherian, both assertions are consequences of (V 5.2.3). In the general case, we must see that every morphism
\[u:F\longrightarrow G\]
of constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaves on \(X\) possesses a kernel and a cokernel.

As regards the cokernel, this follows from (V 3.1.3). Let us prove the existence of the kernel of \(u\). For every noetherian open subset \(V\) of \(X\), we know that the restriction of \(u\) to \(V\)
\[u_V:F_V\longrightarrow G_V\]

admits a kernel in the category \(\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(V)\), admitting the simple description that follows. If \(K\) is the kernel of \(u_V\) in the category \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(V))\), \(K\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler-Artin-Rees condition and, denoting by \(K'\) its projective system of universal images, there exists an integer \(r\) such that the projective system
\[m_r(K')=\bigl(K'_{n+1}/\ell^{n+1}K'_{n+r}\bigr)_{n\in\mathbb N}\]
(with the obvious transition morphisms) is constructible \(\ell\)-adic. The obvious composite below is a kernel of \(u_V\) in the category \(\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(V)\):
\[m_r(K')\longrightarrow K'\longrightarrow K.\]
The preceding construction shows in particular that if \(W\) is an open subset contained in \(V\), the restriction to \(W\) of a kernel for \(u_V\) is a kernel for \(u_W\).

Let then \((V_i)_{i\in I}\) be a covering of \(X\) by noetherian open subsets. If \(i\) and \(j\) are two elements of \(I\), the restrictions to \(V_i\cap V_j\) of the kernels of \(u_{V_i}\) and \(u_{V_j}\) are canonically isomorphic, which allows us, by gluing them, to construct a kernel of \(u\).

Let \(F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) be a constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaf. Since the ring \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) acts in an obvious way on each sheaf \(F_n\), it also acts on \(F\). Moreover, if \(F\) and \(G\) are two constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaves and
\[u:F\longrightarrow G\]

a morphism, \(u\) is compatible with the actions of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) on \(F\) and \(G\) respectively. This shows that \(\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(X)\) is canonically equipped with a structure of abelian \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-category.
This justifies the following terminology:
Terminology: In what follows we shall call constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves the constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaves.
One should take care not to confuse this notion with that of \((\mathbb Z_\ell)_X\)-Module, which will not be used in what follows.
The category \(\ell\text{-}\operatorname{adc}(X)\) will also be denoted
\[\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]
1.2. Constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves

blocks.title: Definition 1.2.1.
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2.1. — A constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf
\[F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\]

is said to be constant-twisted if and only if the sheaves \(F_n\) are locally constant on \(X_{\mathrm{\acute et}}\).

We call the category of constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves the full subcategory of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) generated by the constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves.

blocks.title: Example 1.2.2.
blocks.html: Example 1.2.2. — The \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\).
Suppose the integer \(\ell\) is prime to the residue characteristics of \(X\). For every integer \(n\), raising to the power \(\ell\) defines a morphism of
locally constant sheaves:
\[\mu_{\ell^{n+1}}\longrightarrow\mu_{\ell^n}.\]
This defines in an obvious way a projective system:
\[\mathbb Z_\ell(1)=(\mu_{\ell^{n+1}})_{n\in\mathbb N}.\]
Reducing for each integer \(n\) to the case where \(\mu_{\ell^n}\) and \(\mu_{\ell^{n+1}}\) are constant, we see that the projective system \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\) is \(\ell\)-adic.

When \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\), with \(K\) a field of finite type over the prime field, the \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\) is an example of a constructible constant-twisted \(\ell\)-adic sheaf (1.2.1) for which there exists no finite étale extension \(K'\) of \(K\) such that the inverse images of the \(F_n\) on \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(K')\) are constant. Indeed \(K'\) would contain, for every integer \(n\), a primitive \(\ell^n\)-th root of unity, which is not possible for an extension of finite type of the prime field.
Reminders 1.2.3. (SGA 1 V 7).

We know that the category of locally constant constructible torsion abelian sheaves on a prescheme \(T\) is equivalent to the category of étale coverings of \(T\) equipped with a structure of abelian \(X\)-group. Now let \(X\) be a connected locally noetherian prescheme. Let us denote by \(R_X\) the category of étale coverings of \(X\) and by \(\operatorname{Ensf}\) that of finite sets. If \(a\) is a geometric point of \(X\), let \(F_a\) be the functor:
\[F_a:R_X\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ensf},\]
which to every étale covering of \(X\) associates its geometric fiber at the point \(a\).
The fundamental group
of \(X\) at the point \(a\) being by definition the group of automorphisms of \(F_a\) suitably topologized, the functor \(F_a\) can be interpreted as a functor
\[R_X\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ensf}(\pi_1)\]
from the category \(R_X\) to the category of finite sets equipped with a continuous action of \(\pi_1=\pi_1(X,a)\).
We recall the following proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition 1.2.3.1.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2.3.1. — The functor
\[F_a:R_X\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ensf}(\pi_1)\]

is an equivalence of categories. It commutes with inductive limits and with finite projective limits.
Now let
\[\operatorname{Lc}(X),\quad \operatorname{Abf}(\pi_1)\]

be respectively the category of locally constant constructible torsion abelian sheaves, and that of finite abelian groups equipped with a continuous action of the fundamental group. The functor \(F\) induces an exact functor, still denoted \(F_a\),
\[F_a:\operatorname{Lc}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Abf}(\pi_1),\]
which is an equivalence of categories.
1.2.4. Constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves on a connected prescheme
The reminders of (1.2.3) make the lemma below immediate.

blocks.title: Lemma 1.2.4.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.4.1. — The extension \(\widehat F_a\) of \(F_a\) to the category of projective systems indexed by \(\mathbb N\) induces an equivalence of the category of constructible constant-twisted \(\ell\)-adic sheaves with the category
\[(\ell\mathbb Z)\text{-}\operatorname{ad}(\operatorname{Abf}(\pi_1)).\]
Now let \(F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) be an object of the category
\[(\ell\mathbb Z)\text{-}\operatorname{ad}(\operatorname{Abf}(\pi_1)).\]

Its projective limit \(\varprojlim_{\mathbb N}(F_n)\) is naturally equipped with a structure of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of finite type on which \(\pi_1\) acts continuously for the \(\ell\)-adic topology. Recall that if \(G\) and \(M\) denote respectively a profinite group and a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of finite type, we call a continuous action of \(G\) on \(M\) a continuous map
\[\begin{array}{rcl}G\times M&\longrightarrow&M\\(g,m)&\longmapsto&g.m\end{array}\]
such that:
\[\begin{aligned}(i)\quad &g.(m+n)=g.m+g.n &&(g\in G,\ m\text{ and }n\in M),\\ (ii)\quad &(gh).m=g.(h.m) &&(g\text{ and }h\in G,\ m\in M).\end{aligned}\]
It is then clear that for every \(\ell\)-adic integer \(a\),
\[g.(am)=a(g.m)\qquad(g\in G,\ m\in M).\]
Moreover, the group of invertible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-linear maps from \(M\) to \(M\), denoted \(\operatorname{Gl}(M)\), identifies with the projective limit of the groups

GL\((M/\ell^{n+1}M)\). These latter groups being finite, the group \(GL(M)\) is thus naturally equipped with a structure of profinite group and in particular with a structure of compact topological group. One easily checks that it amounts to the same to give a continuous action of the profinite group \(G\) on \(M\) or to give a morphism of topological groups:
\[G\longrightarrow GL(M),\]
this latter group being equipped with the topology described above.
This said, we define in an obvious way a functor
\[\varprojlim_{\mathbb N}:(\ell\mathbb Z)\text{-}\operatorname{ad}(\operatorname{Abf}(\pi_1))\longrightarrow \mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{modf}(\pi_1),\]
where \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{modf}(\pi_1)\) denotes the category of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type equipped with a continuous action of \(\pi_1\).
This allows us to state the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma 1.2.4.2.
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.4.2. — The preceding functor
\[\varprojlim_{\mathbb N}:(\ell\mathbb Z)\text{-}\operatorname{ad}(\operatorname{Abf}(\pi_1))\longrightarrow \mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{modf}(\pi_1)\]

is an equivalence of categories.
Proof: It is clear that it is generically surjective: a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of finite type \(E\) on which \(\pi_1\) acts continuously is the image of the projective system
\[(E/\ell^{n+1}E)_{n\in\mathbb N},\]

with the obvious actions of \(\pi_1\). Now let \(F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) and \(G=(G_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) be two objects of the category \((\ell\mathbb Z)\text{-}\operatorname{ad}(\operatorname{Abf}(\pi_1))\), and let us denote for simplicity by \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\pi_1}(F,G)\) the set of morphisms from \(F\) to \(G\) in this

category. Forgetting the action of \(\pi_1\), we may consider \(F\) and \(G\) as objects of the category of \(\ell\)-adic projective systems of finite abelian groups, and we denote by \(\operatorname{Hom}_0(F,G)\) the set of morphisms from \(F\) to \(G\) in this category. The group \(\pi_1\) acts on \(\operatorname{Hom}_0(F,G)\) in the following manner: an element \(s\) of \(\pi_1\) defines in a clear way two automorphisms, denoted in the same way, of \(F\) and \(G\) respectively in the category of \(\ell\)-adic projective systems of finite abelian groups, and if \(u\) belongs to \(\operatorname{Hom}_0(F,G)\) we set
\[s.u=s\circ u\circ s^{-1}.\]

Under these conditions, it is clear that \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\pi_1}(F,G)\) identifies canonically with the set of invariants of \(\pi_1\) in \(\operatorname{Hom}_0(F,G)\), and likewise \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\pi_1}(F_n,G_n)\) identifies for every \(n\) with \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(F_n,G_n)^{\pi_1}\), the action of \(\pi_1\) being defined analogously. It follows for example from (SERRE: Groupes proalgébriques, Prop. 12) that, denoting by \(M\) and \(N\) the projective limits of \(F\) and \(G\) respectively, we have
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(M,N)=\varprojlim_n\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z}(F_n,G_n).\]
Taking the invariants of both sides under \(\pi_1\) and using the fact that this operation, corresponding to a projective limit, commutes with projective limits, we deduce the assertion.
Lemmas 1.2.4.1. and 1.2.4.2. allow us to state the following less precise proposition:

blocks.title: Proposition 1.2.5.
blocks.html: Proposition 1.2.5. — The category of constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves on a connected locally noetherian prescheme \(X\) is equivalent to the category of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type on which \(\pi_1(X)\) acts continuously for the \(\ell\)-adic topology.

Remark: Let \(F\) be a constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf and \(M\) the associated \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of finite type equipped with a continuous action of \(\pi_1\). For \(F\) to be locally constant, it is clearly sufficient that \(\pi_1\) act on \(M\) through a finite quotient, which is not always satisfied (1.2.2). It is even necessary when \(X\) is normal. By decomposing \(X\) into connected components if necessary, we may to see this reduce to the case where it is integral. I claim that then, denoting by \(K\) its field of rational functions, if there exists a finite separable extension \(K'\) of \(K\) such that the inverse image \(F_{K'}\) of \(F\) above \(\operatorname{Spec}(K')\) is constant, then the group \(\pi_1(X)\) acts on \(M\) through a finite group. Indeed (SGA 1 V 8.2) \(\pi_1(K)\longrightarrow\pi_1(X)\) is surjective, and the hypothesis means that the kernel of the composite \(\pi_1(K)\longrightarrow\pi_1(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}(M)\) is of finite index. The same therefore holds for the kernel of \(\pi_1(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}(M)\).

blocks.title: Proposition 1.2.6.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2.6. — Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and \(F\) a projective system indexed by \(\mathbb N\) of abelian sheaves on \(X_{\mathrm{ét}}\). The following assertions are equivalent:

(i) \(F\) is a constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf,

(ii) Every noetherian open subset \(U\) of \(X\) is the union of a finite number of locally closed parts \((Z_i)_{1\leq i\leq q}\) above which the inverse image of \(F\) is a constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf,

(iii) \(X\) admits a covering by open subsets, unions of a finite number of locally closed parts, above which the inverse image of \(F\) is a constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf.
Proof: It is clear that (ii) implies (iii).

(iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i): If \(F\) satisfies (iii), its geometric stalks are \(\ell\)-adic, hence \(F\) is \(\ell\)-adic by (SGA 4 VIII 3.6). Moreover, its components are constructible (SGA 4 IX 2.8).

To see that (i) implies (ii), we reduce immediately to the case where \(X\) is noetherian. If \(F\) is constructible \(\ell\)-adic, its strict graded object \(\operatorname{grs}(F)\) is a noetherian \((\operatorname{gr}_{\ell}\mathbb Z,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\)-module (V 5.1.6). Consequently (SGA 4 IX 2.9.) \(X\) is the union of a finite number of locally closed parts \((Z_i)_{1\leq i\leq q}\) above which the inverse image of \(\operatorname{grs}(F)\) is a locally constant sheaf. Since the property for a sheaf with finite stalks of being locally constant is stable under extensions, we deduce that for every integer \(n\), the inverse images on each \(Z_i\) of the component \(F_n\) of \(F\) are locally constant sheaves.
1.3. Operations \(\otimes\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}\) on \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves.
1.3.1. Tensor product.

Let \(F\) and \(G\) be two objects of the category \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\). If \(F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) and \(G=(G_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\), we define the tensor product \(F\otimes G\) of \(F\) and \(G\) by the following formula on objects and in the obvious way on arrows:
\[(F\otimes G)_n=F_n\otimes G_n.\]
More precisely, we define in a clear way a right exact additive bifunctor, called the tensor product:

\[\otimes:\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\times\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X)).\]

blocks.title: Proposition 1.3.2.
blocks.html: Proposition 1.3.2. — If \(F\) and \(G\) are two constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves, the tensor product \(F\otimes G\) is a constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf.

Proof: The \(\ell\)-adicity is clear. Moreover, it follows immediately from the definition of constructible sheaves (SGA 4 IX 2.3.) that a tensor product of two constructible sheaves is constructible.
By restriction, we therefore define a right exact additive bifunctor, again called the tensor product:
\[\otimes:\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\times\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]
1.3.3. Sheaf of homomorphisms.

Let \(F=(F_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) and \(G=(G_n)_{n\in\mathbb N}\) be two constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves. We assume that \(F\) is locally free, by which we mean that for every integer \(n\), the sheaf \(F_n\) is locally free over the constant sheaf \((\mathbb Z/\ell^{n+1}\mathbb Z)_X\). For every integer \(n\), the structural exact sequence:
\[G_{n+1}\xrightarrow{\ell^{n+1}}G_{n+1}\longrightarrow G_n\longrightarrow0\]
furnishes, since \(F_{n+1}\) is locally free over \((\mathbb Z/\ell^{n+1}\mathbb Z)_X\), an exact sequence:
\[(1)\quad \mathcal Hom(F_{n+1},G_{n+1})\xrightarrow{\ell^{n+1}}\mathcal Hom(F_{n+1},G_{n+1})\longrightarrow\mathcal Hom(F_{n+1},G_n)\longrightarrow0.\]
Since \(\ell^{n+1}G_n=0\), the structural exact sequence:
\[F_{n+1}\xrightarrow{\ell^{n+1}}F_{n+1}\longrightarrow F_n\longrightarrow0\]
defines in a clear way an isomorphism:
\[(2)\qquad \mathcal Hom(F_{n+1},G_n)\longleftarrow \mathcal Hom(F_n,G_n).\]
Combining (1) and (2), we obtain for every integer \(n\) a morphism:
\[\mathcal Hom(F_{n+1},G_{n+1})\longrightarrow \mathcal Hom(F_n,G_n).\]
Whence a projective system
\[\mathcal Hom(F,G)=\bigl(\mathcal Hom(F_n,G_n)\bigr)_{n\in\mathbb N},\]
which is clearly a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf.
1.3.4. Generalities on invertible \(\ell\)-adic sheaves. Application to the sheaves \(\mathbb Z_\ell(r)\ (r\in\mathbb Z)\).

Let \(L\) be a constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf. We shall say that it is invertible if for every integer \(n\) its \(n^{\text{th}}\) component \(L_n\) is an invertible \((\mathbb Z/\ell^{n+1}\mathbb Z)_X\)-Module, i.e. locally free of rank one. It amounts to the same to say that \(L\) is locally free in the sense of the preceding paragraph and that the geometric stalks of the \(L_n\) are nonzero cyclic abelian groups.
If \(L\) is an invertible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf, we agree to set
\[L^{-1}=\mathcal Hom(L,\mathbb Z_\ell).\]
It is clear that we have an isomorphism
\[L\otimes L^{-1}\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathbb Z_\ell,\]

deduced from the analogous isomorphism component by component (EGA 0I 5.4.3.1). Following then (EGA 0I 5.4.4), we agree to set \(L^{\otimes0}=\mathbb Z_\ell\) and for \(n\geq1\), \(L^{\otimes-n}=(L^{-1})^{\otimes n}\). With these notations, there then exists a canonical functorial isomorphism
\[L^{\otimes m}\otimes L^{\otimes n}\xrightarrow{\sim}L^{\otimes(m+n)}\]
for all rational integers \(m\) and \(n\). Moreover this isomorphism enjoys
the usual associativity properties.
In particular, the graded objects
\[\bigl(L^{\otimes i}\bigr)_{i\in\mathbb N}\quad\text{and}\quad \bigl(L^{\otimes i}\bigr)_{i\in\mathbb Z}\]
are \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-Algebras, graded respectively by \(\mathbb N\) and \(\mathbb Z\).
Example: The \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\) defined in (1.2.2) is invertible, and we shall agree in what follows to set:
\[\mathbb Z_\ell(r)=\bigl(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\bigr)^{\otimes r}\qquad(r\in\mathbb Z).\]

pages.title: 1.4. Various categories constructed from the category of constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves.

blocks.html: 1.4. Various categories constructed from the category of constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves.
1.4.1. Constructible \(A\)-sheaves \((A,\ \text{finite }\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-algebra})\).
Let \(A\) be a finite \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-algebra. We define a category denoted
\[A\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X),\]
whose objects are called constructible \(A\)-sheaves, in the following manner.
(i) An object of \(A\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) is a pair \((F,u)\) consisting of a constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(F\) and a morphism of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-algebras:
\[u:A\longrightarrow\operatorname{End}(F).\]
(ii) If \((F,u)\) and \((F',u')\) are two constructible \(A\)-sheaves, an arrow \((F,u)\to(F',u')\) is a morphism of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves
\[f:F\longrightarrow F'\]
such that for every element \(a\) of \(A\) the diagram below is commutative:
\[\xymatrix{F\ar[r]^f\ar[d]_{u(a)}&F'\ar[d]^{u'(a)}\\F\ar[r]_f&F'.}\]

We shall call a constant-twisted constructible \(A\)-sheaf a constructible \(A\)-sheaf whose underlying constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf is constant-twisted, and the category of constructible constant-twisted \(A\)-sheaves, the full subcategory of the category of constructible \(A\)-sheaves generated by the constructible constant-twisted \(A\)-sheaves.

It follows immediately from (1.2.4) that if \(X\) is connected, the category of constructible constant-twisted \(A\)-sheaves is equivalent to the category of \(A\)-modules of finite type equipped with a continuous and \(A\)-linear action of \(\pi_1(X)\) on the underlying \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of finite type (1.2.4).
1.4.2. Constructible \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves.

It is clear that the full subcategory of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) generated by the constructible torsion \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves (i.e. those annihilated by a power of \(\ell\)) is thick.

blocks.title: Definition 1.4.3.
blocks.html:
Definition 1.4.3. We call the category of constructible \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves on \(X\), and denote by
\[\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\]

the abelian quotient category (cf. Gabriel's thesis) of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) by the thick abelian subcategory of torsion \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves. An object of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) is called a constructible \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf.

The category \(\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) admits the following simple description. It has the same set of objects as \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) and if \(F\) and \(G\) are two such objects,
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Q_\ell}(F,G)=\mathbb Q_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(F,G),\]

where \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(F,G)\) and \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Q_\ell}(F,G)\) denote respectively the morphisms in \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) and \(\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\). Indeed, if
\[u:\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow D\]

denotes an exact functor from \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) to an abelian category \(D\), it amounts to the same to say that \(u\) makes the multiplications by elements of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) invertible, or that it vanishes on the torsion objects, which shows immediately that the (abelian) category described by objects and morphisms above is a solution of the universal problem of passage to the quotient.

We say that a constructible \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf is constant-twisted if it is the image of a constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf. We call the category of constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves the full subcategory of
\[\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\]
generated by the constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves.

It follows from (1.2.4) that if \(X\) is connected, the category of constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves is equivalent to the category of finite-dimensional \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-vector spaces \(V\), equipped with a continuous action of \(\pi_1(X)\), i.e. with a continuous map
\[u:\pi_1(X)\times V\longrightarrow V\]
satisfying the properties made explicit in (1.2.4), which implies in particular that \(u(g,v)\) is \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-linear in \(v\) for every \(g\in\pi_1(X)\).
It is immediate to see that it amounts to the same to give a morphism of topological groups \(\pi_1(X)\to GL(V)\), the latter being equipped with its
natural topology of \(\ell\)-adic analytic group.
1.4.3. Constructible \(L\)-sheaves \((L\text{ a finite }\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-algebra})\).

Let \(L\) be a finite \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-algebra. Analogously to what was done in (1.4.1) starting from the constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves, we define, starting from the category of constructible \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves, that of constructible \(L\)-sheaves, then that of constructible constant-twisted \(L\)-sheaves. If \(X\) is connected, the category of constructible constant-twisted \(L\)-sheaves is equivalent to the category of finite-dimensional \(L\)-vector spaces equipped with a continuous action of \(\pi_1(X)\), i.e. with a continuous action of \(\pi_1(X)\) on the underlying \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-vector space, commuting with the action of \(L\).
If \(A\) is a finite commutative \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-algebra and \(F\) and \(G\) are two constructible \(A\)-sheaves, the projective system
\[F\otimes_A G=\bigl(F_n\otimes_{A_n}G_n\bigr)_{n\in\mathbb N},\]

with the obvious transition morphisms, is a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf. Moreover \(A\) acts in an obvious way on each of its components, which allows us to equip it naturally with a structure of \(A\)-sheaf. We thus define a right exact bifunctor, called the tensor product
\[A\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\times A\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow A\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]

Now let \(F\) and \(G\) be two \(A\)-sheaves and suppose that \(F\) is locally free, i.e. that for every integer \(n\), \(F_n\) is a locally free \((A/\ell^{n+1}A)_X\)-Module. Then, just as in (1.3.3), we
see that the projective system
\[\mathcal Hom_A(F,G)=\bigl(\mathcal Hom_{A_n}(F_n,G_n)\bigr)_{n\in\mathbb N},\]
with the obvious transition morphisms, is a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf. Moreover, since \(A\) acts naturally on its components, it is canonically equipped with a structure of \(A\)-sheaf.

Just as in (1.3.3), we define the notion of invertible \(A\)-sheaf and, if \(L\) is such an \(A\)-sheaf, we give a meaning to the expression \(L^{\otimes r}\) for every natural integer \(r\). Paragraph (1.3.3) adapts without difficulty to this situation.
Let \(F\) be a constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf, and let us denote by \(p\) the canonical functor
\[\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]
The composite functor of \(p\) and the functor \(G\longmapsto F\otimes G\) makes the multiplications by elements of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) invertible and consequently defines a functor
\[\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X),\]
which is right exact. Applying the same reasoning to the first argument, we thus define a right exact bifunctor, called the tensor product:
\[\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\times\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]

If \(X\) is connected and \(F\) and \(G\) are two constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves corresponding to representations of \(\pi_1(X)\) on the vector spaces \(V\) and \(W\) respectively, the tensor product \(F\otimes G\) is again locally constant and corresponds to the diagonal representation of \(\pi_1(X)\) on \(V\otimes W\).

Let \(F\) be a locally free constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf (1.3.3). The composite functor of \(p\) and the functor \(G\longmapsto\mathcal Hom(F,G)\) (1.3.2) makes the multiplications by the elements of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) invertible and consequently defines a functor
\[\mathcal Hom(F,\mathbin\cdot):\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]
If \(F\) and \(F'\) are two locally free constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves defining isomorphic \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves, there exists a morphism of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves
\[u:F\longrightarrow F'\]

whose kernel and cokernel are torsion. This shows at once that the functors \(\mathcal Hom(F,\mathbin\cdot)\) and \(\mathcal Hom(F',\mathbin\cdot)\) from \(\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) to itself are isomorphic.

Now let \(F\) be a constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf. If we again denote by \(F\) the corresponding \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf, it follows, from the interpretation of the restrictions of \(F\) to the connected components \(Y\) of \(X\) as continuous \(\pi_1(Y)\)-modules, that \(F\) can be written uniquely up to isomorphism in the form
\[F=P\oplus T,\]

with \(P\) locally free and \(T\) torsion. This allows us to define, for every \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf \(G\), a \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaf \(\mathcal Hom(F,G)=\mathcal Hom(P,G)\). An isomorphism \(u:F\longrightarrow F'\) of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves defines for every \(G\) an isomorphism
\[\mathcal Hom(F',G)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathcal Hom(F,G).\]
More precisely we thus define a bifunctor
\[\mathcal Hom:\bigl(\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fct}(X)\bigr)^\circ\times\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X),\]
denoting by \(\mathbb Q_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fct}(X)\) the category of constructible constant-twisted \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves. This bifunctor possesses the usual exactness properties.

Now let \(L\) be an extension of finite type of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\), and let us denote by \(A\) the integral closure of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) in \(L\), so that \(A\) is a free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-algebra of finite type and a Dedekind ring. The category of constructible \(L\)-sheaves is isomorphic to the quotient category of the category of constructible \(A\)-sheaves by that of constructible torsion \(A\)-sheaves. This allows us, just as in (1.4.6), to define from the tensor product of (1.4.4) a tensor product:
\[L\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\times L\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow L\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]

As the ring \(A\) is a Dedekind ring, the argument used in (1.4.6) shows that every twisted constant \(A\)-sheaf is a direct sum of a locally free \(A\)-sheaf and a torsion \(A\)-sheaf. Denoting then by \(L\text{-}\operatorname{fct}(X)\) the category of constructible twisted constant \(L\)-sheaves, we define just as in (1.4.6) a bifunctor
\[\mathcal Hom:\bigl(L\text{-}\operatorname{fct}(X)\bigr)^\circ\times L\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow L\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\]
satisfying the usual exactness properties.
1.5. Constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves.

blocks.title: Definition 1.5.1.
blocks.html: Definition 1.5.1. Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme. An object \(F\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\) is called a constructible \((\operatorname{AR}\text{-}\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf if \(X\) admits a covering
by noetherian open sets such that the restriction of \(F\) above each \(U_i\) is a \((\operatorname{AR},\ell\mathbb Z)\)-noetherian object (5 1.3).

It follows immediately from (V 5.2.1) that the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\) generated by the constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves is stable under kernels and cokernels. In particular, it is an abelian category.
1.5.2. Constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves which are locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null.

It is clear that a \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null object (V 2.2.1) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\) is a constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf if and only if its components are constructible. It follows in particular from (V 2.2.2) and (SGA 4 IX 2.6) that the category of constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves which are \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null is a thick abelian subcategory of the full category generated by the constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves in \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\).

Agreeing to say that an object \(P\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\) is locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null if there exists an open covering of \(X\) for the étale topology (or, what amounts to the same thing, for the Zariski topology) above each open set of which \(P\) is \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null, we see, by reducing by localization to the preceding case, that the category of constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves which are locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null is a thick abelian subcategory of that of the constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves.

We observe that if \(X\) is noetherian, the constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves are the noetherian \((\operatorname{AR},\ell\mathbb Z)\)-adic objects of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\), and locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null is equivalent to \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null.

blocks.title: Definition 1.5.3.
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5.3. Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme. We call category of constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves on \(X\), and we denote by
\[(\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X),\]

the quotient abelian category of the full subcategory generated by the constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves in

\[\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\]

by the full, thick subcategory generated by the constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves which are locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null.

Whenever it may be useful, we shall denote by \(U(X)\) the full subcategory of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\) generated by the constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves.
A constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf being in particular a constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf, we define in an evident way a functor:
\[u:\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow(\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X).\]

blocks.title: Proposition 1.5.5.
blocks.html: Proposition 1.5.5. The functor \(u\) above is an equivalence of categories.
Proof: To prove that \(u\) is fully faithful and essentially surjective, we reduce "by gluing" (as in the proof of 1.1.3) to the analogous statement (V 5.1.3).
2. Formalism of \(\ell\)-adic cohomology.
2.1. Inverse image.
Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of locally noetherian preschemes. The inverse image functor
\[f^*:\operatorname{Ab}(Y)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ab}(X)\]
is exact and therefore extends in an evident way to an exact functor, denoted in the same manner,
\[f^*:\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(Y))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X)).\]

This last functor evidently transforms constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves into constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves, and locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems into locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems. With the notation of (1.5.4), it therefore induces two "inverse image" functors
\[f^*:\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Y)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X),\] \[f^*:U(Y)\longrightarrow U(X),\]
the second being exact.

The equivalence of categories (1.5.5) and the fact that \(f^*\) transforms locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems into locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems then show that the inverse image functor
\[f^*:\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Y)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\]
is also exact.
If \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) is another morphism of locally noetherian preschemes, the functors \(f^*\) and \(g^*\) are evidently related by an isomorphism
\[(g\circ f)^*\xrightarrow{\sim}f^*\circ g^*,\]
with the usual "associativity" property (SGA 1 VI 7 ff.)
2.2. Higher direct images.
Let
\[f:X\longrightarrow Y\]
be a separated morphism of finite type of locally noetherian preschemes. We recall the following result of étale cohomology (SGA 4 XVII; for the proper case cf. SGA 4 XIV 1.1):

blocks.title: Lemma 2.2.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.1. : Let \(A\) be a commutative ring which is annihilated by a nonzero integer \(n\), and let \(M\) be a constructible \((A,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\)-module. For every integer \(i\), the sheaf
\[R_!^i f(M)\]

is a constructible \((A,\operatorname{Ab}(Y))\)-module.
The following lemma results from this by applying (V 5.3.1) after restriction above the noetherian open sets of \(Y\):

blocks.title: Lemma 2.2.2.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.2. : For every integer \(i\) and every constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), the projective system
\[R_!^i f(F)\]

is a constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf.
This being said, the exact \(\partial\)-functor
\[(R_!^if)_{i\in\mathbb Z}:\operatorname{Ab}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ab}(Y)\]
extends in an evident way to an exact \(\partial\)-functor, denoted likewise,
\[\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(Y)),\]
which by restriction yields an exact \(\partial\)-functor
\[U(X)\longrightarrow U(Y).\tag{1}\]
Moreover, the functors \(R_!^if\) transform locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems into locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems, so that by passage to the
quotient, the \(\partial\)-functor (1) yields an exact \(\partial\)-functor
\[(R_!^if)_{i\in\mathbb Z}:(\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow(\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Y).\]
Using now the equivalence of categories (1.5.5), this last \(\partial\)-functor can be interpreted as an exact \(\partial\)-functor
\[\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Y).\]

blocks.title: Definition 2.2.3.
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2.3. : The functor:
\[R_!^if:\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Y)\]

is called the \(i^{\text{th}}\) direct image with proper supports functor of \(f\).

Remark: Assuming the analogue of lemma (2.2.1) proved for the ordinary higher direct images when \(X\) and \(Y\) are excellent (which is done in characteristic zero (SGA 4 XIX 5.1)), we define in the same way in this case higher direct images in \(\ell\)-adic cohomology. We could likewise develop in this setting the result of the following section.

blocks.title: Proposition 2.2.4.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.4. (Spectral sequence of LERAY). Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) and \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) be two separated morphisms of finite type of locally noetherian preschemes and \(F\) a constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf (resp. \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf). There exists in \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Z)\) a biregular spectral sequence (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 11.1.3) functorial in \(F\):
\[R_!^i g\bigl(R_!^j f(F)\bigr)\Longrightarrow R_!^{i+j}(g\circ f)(F).\]

Proof: By the equivalence of categories (1.5.5) and the way in which the higher direct images are defined for constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves, it suffices to prove the analogous statement for constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves. For every abelian category \(C\), let us agree to denote by

\[\operatorname{Sp}(C)\]

the category of spectral sequences starting at degree 2 with values in \(C\). Associating to every object \(F\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\) the ordinary LERAY spectral sequence, obtained by extension to projective systems,

\[R_!^i g\bigl(R_!^j f(F)\bigr)\Longrightarrow R_!^{i+j}(g\circ f)(F),\tag{S}\]

it is clear that we obtain a functor

\[\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sp}\bigl(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(Z))\bigr),\]

which by restriction yields, by (2.2.1) and the fact that \(U(Z)\) is thick in \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(Z))\), a functor:

\[U(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sp}(U(Z)).\tag{1}\]

The canonical functor \(U(Z)\longrightarrow(\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Z)\) being exact, we deduce from (1) an additive functor:

\[E:U(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sp}\bigl((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Z)\bigr).\tag{2}\]

For every object \(F\) of \(\operatorname{Hom}(\mathbb N^\circ,\operatorname{Ab}(X))\), the associated spectral sequence \(S\) is biregular: this follows from the analogous assertion for the components of the projective system \(F\) and from the fact that the spectral sequence under consideration is supported in the first quadrant. This shows that for every object of \(U(X)\), the corresponding spectral sequence is biregular.

Moreover, if \(u:F\longrightarrow G\) is a morphism of \(U(X)\) whose kernel and cokernel are locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null, it follows from the fact that the higher direct images transform locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems into locally \(\operatorname{AR}\)-null projective systems that \(E(u)\) is an isomorphism on the terms \(E_2^{pq}\); it is therefore an isomorphism, since the spectral sequences under consideration are biregular. We deduce that (2) defines by passage to the quotient a functor

\[(\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Sp}\bigl((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Z)\bigr),\tag{3}\]

associating precisely to every constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf \(F\) a biregular spectral sequence:

\[R_!^i f\bigl(R_!^j g(F)\bigr)\Longrightarrow R_!^{i+j}(g\circ f)(F).\]
2.2.3. Other properties.

A) Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a separated morphism of finite type of locally noetherian preschemes. We suppose that the relative dimension of \(f\) is less than or equal to an integer \(d\). Then for every constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaf \(F\),
\[R_!^i f(F)=0\qquad\text{for }i>2d.\]
This follows from the analogous assertion for the components of \(F\) (SGA 4 X 4.1. and 5.2.).
B) Proper base change.
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} X'&\xrightarrow{g'}&X\\ {\scriptstyle f'}\downarrow&&\downarrow{\scriptstyle f}\\ Y'&\xrightarrow{g}&Y \end{array} \]

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) and \(g:Y'\longrightarrow Y\) be morphisms of locally noetherian preschemes, with \(f\) separated of finite type. Let \(X'=X\times_Y Y'\) (which is locally noetherian since \(f\) is of finite type) and
\[f':X'\longrightarrow Y',\qquad g':X'\longrightarrow X\]

be the canonical morphisms. For every integer \(i\) and every constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(F\), there exists an isomorphism, functorial in \(F\), in \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Y')\):
\[w_i:g^*\bigl(R_!^if(F)\bigr)\longrightarrow R_!^if'\bigl(g'^*F\bigr).\]

Moreover if \(0\longrightarrow F'\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow F''\longrightarrow0\) is an exact sequence of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\) (resp. \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(X)\)), \((w_i)_{i\in\mathbb N}\) commutes with the boundary operators, in other words the diagrams below are commutative:

Proof: It suffices to prove the analogous assertion for constructible \((\operatorname{AR},\mathbb Z_\ell)\)-sheaves. Now this is deduced by passage to the quotient from the analogous assertion for projective systems of torsion sheaves, which is an immediate consequence of the proper base change theorem (cf SGA 4 XII 5.1 in the proper case and SGA 4 XVII in the general case).
C) Exact sequence relative to an open set and its complement.

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a compactifiable morphism of locally noetherian preschemes, \(U\) an open set of \(X\) and \(Z\) the complementary closed set. There exists for every constructible \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(F\) an exact sequence, functorial in \(F\), in \(\mathbb Z_\ell\text{-}\operatorname{fc}(Y)\):
\[ \cdots\longrightarrow R_!^if_U(F/U)\longrightarrow R_!^if(F)\longrightarrow R_!^if_Z(F/Z) \longrightarrow R_!^{i+1}f_U(F/U)\longrightarrow\cdots \]
Proof: Analogous to that of B).

The formalism developed in this section for \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves extends without difficulty to \(A\)-sheaves (\(A\) a finite \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-algebra) and to \(L\)-sheaves (\(L\) a finite extension of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)). As regards for example the case of \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-sheaves, the fundamental point for the proofs is that
all the functors considered for \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves transform torsion \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves into torsion \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves.

pages.title: 3. \(\ell\)-adic cohomology class associated to a cycle.

blocks.html: 3. \(\ell\)-adic cohomology class associated to a cycle.

If \(X\) is a scheme which is proper, or smooth and of finite type, over an algebraically closed field \(k\), we know that for every constructible sheaf \(M\), abelian and of torsion, prime to the characteristic of \(k\), the cohomology groups \(H^i(X,M)\) are abelian groups of finite length. This allows us (2.2.1. Remark) to define for every \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf \(F\) (\(\ell\) prime to the characteristic of \(k\)) a noetherian \((\operatorname{AR},\ell)\)-adic projective system of abelian groups

with the usual properties. In particular, the method used in no. 2 allows us to define the cup-product, the inverse image homomorphism for a morphism of schemes over \(k\), the direct image homomorphism for a proper morphism of smooth schemes over \(k\) (cf. Exp. IV for the definitions in the case of torsion sheaves).

Moreover, we know (1.2.4) that the category of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaves over an algebraically closed field is equivalent to the category of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type, the equivalence being given by the projective limit functor. It will be convenient in practice to identify the \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of finite type \(H^i(X,F)\) with the corresponding \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf.

Let \(\ell\) be a prime number \(\ne\operatorname{car}.k\), and \(X\) a smooth scheme of finite type over \(k\). If \(Z\) is an algebraic cycle of codimension \(d\) of \(X\), we have associated to it in Exp. IV,(*) for every integer \(n\) a cohomology class
\[\gamma_X^{(n)}(Z)\in H^{2d}\bigl(X,(\mu_X^{(n)})^{\otimes d}\bigr),\]

where \(\mu_X^{(n)}\) denotes the locally free \((\mathbb Z/\ell^{n+1}\mathbb Z)_X\)-Module of \(\ell^{n+1}\)-th roots of unity. Moreover, we have seen that if \(n'\geq n\), \(\gamma_X^{(n)}(Y)\) is the image of \(\gamma_X^{(n')}(Y)\) in \(H^{2d}(X,(\mu_X^{(n)})^{\otimes d})\) by the morphism
\[H^{2d}\bigl(X,(\mu_X^{(n')})^{\otimes d}\bigr)\longrightarrow H^{2d}\bigl(X,(\mu_X^{(n)})^{\otimes d}\bigr)\]
deduced from raising to the \(\ell^{n'-n}:\mu_X^{(n')}\longrightarrow\mu_X^{(n)}\)-th power.
We thus associate to \(Z\) an element, denoted \(\gamma_X(Z)\):
\[\gamma_X(Z)\in H^{2d}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(d)),\]
where \(\mathbb Z_\ell(d)\) is defined in (1.3.4). More generally, if \(Z\) is a cycle of \(X\), we associate to it by linearity an \(\ell\)-adic cohomology class in
\[\bigoplus_d H^{2d}(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(d)),\]
which is in fact the projective limit of the cohomology classes associated to \(Z\) in the \(\bigoplus_d H^{2d}(X,(\mu_X^{(n)})^{\otimes d})\).
By passage to the limit, it is evident that the \(\ell\)-adic cohomology classes of the preceding section satisfy the same formal properties as the cohomology classes studied in Exp. IV, namely:
(i) If \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) is a proper morphism of smooth preschemes over \(k\) and \(Z\) a cycle on \(X\), we have:
\[f_*\bigl(\gamma_X(Z)\bigr)=\gamma_Y(f_*(Z)).\tag{*}\]
(ii) If \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) is a morphism of smooth preschemes over \(k\) and \(Z\) a cycle on \(Y\), we have:
\[\gamma_X(f^*Z)=f^*\bigl(\gamma_Y(Z)\bigr).\tag{**}\]
(iii) If \(X\) is a smooth prescheme over \(k\) and \(Z_1\) and \(Z_2\) two cycles on \(X\), we have:
\[\gamma_X(Z_1\cdot Z_2)=\gamma_X(Z_1)\cdot\gamma_X(Z_2),\tag{**}\]
the dot on the left-hand side denoting the intersection product of cycles, and that on the right-hand side the cup-product in cohomology.
(iv) Two algebraically equivalent cycles on a smooth \(k\)-prescheme have the same \(\ell\)-adic cohomology class.

footnotes.html:
This compatibility is not proved in (SGA 4<sup>1/2</sup> Cycle) but
is not difficult to deduce from the results of (loc. cit.).

In the non-excedentary case, see (SGA 4<sup>1/2</sup> Cycle 2.3.8) for
a precise statement.
